chapters.label: Full lesson narration
chapters.script:
Ten small manufacturers each hold invoices from reliable customers. Individually, none has enough receivables to issue a capital-markets security efficiently. Together, they could supply a large and varied pool. The puzzle is how they share funding without pretending that every seller, invoice, and servicing system is identical.

Picture a covered market with one purchasing desk. Sellers bring eligible invoices to the desk. The desk checks them, purchases approved assets through a dedicated vehicle, and finances the combined inventory. New invoices can arrive as old invoices are paid. The sellers remain distinct, but the funding channel is shared.

That is the intuition behind a securitisation conduit. A conduit is a limited-purpose financing vehicle or program that acquires or finances eligible assets, often from multiple sellers, under common funding and administrative arrangements. Many conduits issue short-term asset-backed commercial paper while holding receivables or securities that mature later.

The shared channel solves scale. A manufacturer with twenty crore rupees of invoices may be too small for a standalone term transaction. A conduit can combine its assets with those of other sellers, apply standard eligibility rules, and access a wider investor base. Common funding lowers repetition, but it also creates common points of dependence.

Follow one invoice for two lakh rupees. A component maker has delivered goods to an automobile company and is entitled to payment in sixty days. The conduit purchases or finances that receivable under the program documents. Investor money reaches the conduit, funding reaches the seller, and the automobile company's later payment returns cash to the conduit.

If the conduit finances itself with commercial paper maturing in thirty days, the invoice and the funding do not mature together. The program expects to issue new paper when the first paper matures. That is rollover: fresh short-term funding repays maturing short-term funding while longer-dated assets remain outstanding.

Rollover works quietly in normal markets. It becomes visible when investors refuse to buy new paper. The automobile company may still be entirely capable of paying on day sixty, yet the conduit owes investors on day thirty. Good collateral quality does not eliminate a timing gap between asset cash and liability cash.

A liquidity facility can bridge that gap under specified conditions. A bank may commit to fund eligible assets when commercial-paper markets are unavailable. The facility protects against market timing and rollover pressure, not every form of credit deterioration. Documents commonly limit what can be drawn, when, and against which assets.

Credit enhancement addresses a different problem. Overcollateralisation, reserves, seller interests, guarantees, or subordinated support may absorb losses when obligors fail or receivables become ineligible. Liquidity supplies cash when timing breaks. Credit enhancement absorbs value loss. Treating them as interchangeable hides the exact risk each protection can and cannot cover.

The seller also matters after the receivable enters. It may continue servicing, maintain records, process disputes, and replace ineligible receivables. A weak seller can create bad data, slow collections, or dilution even when the underlying customers remain solvent. Multi-seller does not mean seller risk disappears; it means several seller risks coexist inside one funding program.

Eligibility criteria form the entrance gate. They can restrict overdue invoices, related-party exposures, concentrations, currencies, maturities, documentation defects, or receivables subject to offset. A concentration limit may prevent one obligor from dominating the entire conduit. Yet diversification measured by seller count can be false comfort if every seller supplies the same industry to the same end customers.

Now imagine five sellers all provide parts to one automobile group. The conduit carries many invoices and several seller names, but one corporate buyer still drives the cash. Or imagine sellers in different industries use the same servicing platform and collection bank. The assets are diverse while operations remain concentrated.

The program administrator tests eligibility, calculates borrowing bases, monitors concentrations, manages funding, and coordinates transaction parties. The trustee or collateral agent controls specified rights and accounts. Investors rely on these functions because the conduit is designed to do very little on its own. Limited purpose reduces unrelated business risk but increases reliance on contracted infrastructure.

Some conduits purchase assets directly. Others make loans secured by receivable pools or purchase interests in seller-specific vehicles. The exact legal chain changes who owns the receivable, which entity owes the funding, and what remedy applies after default. The word conduit describes the channel; it does not answer the ownership question.

Ask what happens when an invoice becomes disputed after purchase. The borrowing base may exclude it. The seller may have to repurchase or replace it if a representation was breached. Enhancement may absorb a loss if the obligation cannot be restored. A repurchase promise is useful only if the seller has both a contractual duty and the capacity to perform it.

Then allow the commercial-paper market to close. Maturing investors want cash, new investors do not arrive, and the underlying invoices have not yet paid. The program draws liquidity if the conditions are met, stops purchasing new assets, redirects collections toward repayment, or enters an amortisation mode. A funding event changes the conduit even when no customer has defaulted.

The financial crisis record showed how asset-backed commercial-paper markets could contract when investors doubted collateral, sponsors, and liquidity support. That history demonstrates the mechanism of rollover dependence and confidence-sensitive funding. It does not prove that every conduit shares the same assets, protections, or failure path.

For an Indian analysis, Reserve Bank of India directions and the applicable transfer, securitisation, and prudential rules establish the legal and regulatory boundaries. A credit conclusion still requires the program documents, seller schedules, asset data, liquidity agreement, enhancement terms, maturity ladder, concentration reports, and trigger definitions.

Suppose the conduit holds one hundred crore rupees of receivables and has ninety crore of commercial paper outstanding. The ten-crore difference may appear to be protection. But its usefulness depends on whether receivables remain eligible, whether dilution reduces balances, whether liquidity can be drawn, when paper matures, and which claims rank ahead. One subtraction cannot replace the cash timeline.

Perspective changes the meaning of the arrangement. To a small seller, the conduit is working-capital funding. To a commercial-paper investor, it is a short-dated claim. To the liquidity bank, it is a contingent obligation. To the administrator, it is a daily test of eligibility, concentration, and maturities. Each party sees the same vehicle through a different clock.

The same funding problem appears among rooftop-solar installers. Several installers lease panels to households and sell eligible rental receivables into a shared program. The variety of cities may reduce local concentration, but all installers may depend on one billing platform or one panel supplier. The analyst must look for the common dependency hiding behind the visible diversity.

The conduit earns its place when it gives small pools disciplined access to common funding. It becomes fragile when short liabilities, shared infrastructure, and correlated assets are mistaken for diversification simply because many sellers stand at the same desk.
